content.body: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity. The principle of conservation of momentum states that total momentum in an isolated system is constant.

Momentum: p = mv  (kg·m/s)
Impulse: J = FΔt = Δp

Conservation: m₁u₁ + m₂u₂ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂
(elastic: KE conserved; inelastic: KE not conserved)

content.title: Collision Example
content.body:
Ball A (2kg, 3m/s) hits stationary Ball B (2kg).
Elastic collision:
m₁u₁ = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂
2×3 = 2v₁ + 2v₂
With KE conservation: v₁=0, v₂=3 m/s
Ball A stops, Ball B moves at 3m/s

questions.question: A 5kg object moving at 4m/s has momentum:
questions.explanation: p = mv = 5 × 4 = 20 kg·m/s

questions.question: Impulse equals change in:
questions.explanation: Impulse J = FΔt = Δp

questions.question: In a perfectly elastic collision:
questions.explanation: Elastic collision conserves both momentum AND kinetic energy

questions.question: Objects that stick together after collision undergo:
questions.explanation: When objects stick together, maximum KE is lost — perfectly inelastic

questions.question: A 3kg object accelerates from 2m/s to 8m/s. The impulse is:
What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

content.body:
Angular quantities:
Angular displacement θ (radians)
Angular velocity ω = dθ/dt (rad/s)
Angular acceleration α = dω/dt (rad/s²)

Linear ↔ Angular:
v = ωr, a = αr, s = θr

Rotational equations (mirror of SUVAT):
ω = ω₀ + αt
θ = ω₀t + ½αt²
ω² = ω₀² + 2αθ

Torque: τ = Iα (like F=ma)
Moment of Inertia I = Σmr²

content.title: Rotational KE
content.body:
Rotational KE = ½Iω²
Total KE of rolling object = ½mv² + ½Iω²
For solid sphere: I = 2/5 mr²

questions.question: Angular velocity unit is...
questions.explanation: Angular velocity ω is measured in radians per second (rad/s).

questions.question: Relation between linear and angular velocity: v =
questions.explanation: Linear velocity v = ωr, where r is the radius and ω is angular velocity.

questions.question: Torque τ = Iα is analogous to...
questions.explanation: τ=Iα mirrors F=ma. Torque replaces force, moment of inertia replaces mass.

questions.question: Rotational KE =
questions.explanation: Rotational kinetic energy = ½Iω², analogous to ½mv² for linear KE.

questions.question: A wheel starts from rest with α=2 rad/s². After 3s, ω =
questions.explanation: ω = ω₀+αt = 0+2×3 = 6 rad/s

questions.question: Moment of inertia depends on...
questions.explanation: I = Σmr² — depends on how mass is distributed relative to the rotation axis.

questions.question: For solid sphere: I =
questions.explanation: The moment of inertia of a solid sphere about its diameter is I = 2mr²/5.

questions.question: Angular displacement in 4s starting from rest with α=3 rad/s² is...
questions.explanation: θ = ½αt² = ½×3×16 = 24 rad

questions.question: A rolling ball has both translational and rotational KE. Total KE =
questions.explanation: A rolling object has translational KE (½mv²) from its centre of mass motion PLUS rotational KE (½Iω²).

questions.question: Torque is largest when force is applied...
questions.explanation: τ = Fr sinθ. Maximum when θ=90° (perpendicular). This is why door handles are at the edge.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
SHM: acceleration is proportional to displacement and directed towards equilibrium
a = −ω²x

Displacement: x = A cos(ωt + φ)
Velocity: v = −Aω sin(ωt + φ)
Amplitude A = maximum displacement
Period T = 2π/ω, Frequency f = 1/T

Energy in SHM:
KE = ½mω²(A²−x²)
PE = ½mω²x²
Total E = ½mω²A² = constant

At x=0 (equilibrium): maximum KE, zero PE
At x=±A (extremes): zero KE, maximum PE

content.title: Pendulum
content.body:
Simple pendulum: T = 2π√(L/g)
For small angles only
T independent of mass and amplitude!

Mass-spring: T = 2π√(m/k)
k = spring constant (N/m)

questions.question: In SHM, acceleration is proportional to...
questions.explanation: SHM: a = −ω²x. Acceleration is proportional to displacement and directed towards equilibrium (opposite direction).

questions.question: Period of simple pendulum T =
questions.explanation: Period of a simple pendulum depends on length L and gravity g. T = 2π√(L/g).

questions.question: In SHM, maximum KE occurs at...
questions.explanation: At equilibrium (x=0), all energy is kinetic. KE = ½mω²A² is maximum.

questions.question: Total energy in SHM is...
questions.explanation: Total mechanical energy in SHM = ½mω²A² = constant. It just converts between KE and PE.

questions.question: Period of pendulum depends on...
questions.explanation: Surprisingly, the pendulum period T=2π√(L/g) is independent of mass and amplitude (for small angles).

questions.question: Spring-mass system: k doubles, same mass. Period...
questions.explanation: T=2π√(m/k). If k doubles, T = T/√2. Stiffer spring → faster oscillation.

questions.question: At maximum displacement in SHM, velocity is...
questions.explanation: At the extremes (x=±A), the object momentarily stops before returning. v=0.

questions.question: x = A cos(ωt). The angular frequency ω =
questions.explanation: ω = 2πf = 2π/T, measured in rad/s.

questions.question: A pendulum has L=1m. Period on Earth (g=9.8) ≈
SHM requires a restoring force that...
options: Increases with displacement away from equilibrium
questions.answer: Increases with displacement away from equilibrium
questions.explanation: The restoring force F=−kx increases with displacement, pulling the object back to equilibrium.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Advanced Mechanics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Advanced Mechanics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Advanced Mechanics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Advanced Mechanics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Advanced Mechanics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Advanced Mechanics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Advanced Mechanics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
